title: Build a Website
tagline: Ship a real, multi-page website from scratch using Claude Code. Deploy it to the internet with your name on it.
outcome: When you finish this project you will have a live website at a real URL. Not a template, not a drag-and-drop page. Real code, deployed to the real internet, built by you through a conversation with Claude.
submission_prompt: Paste the public URL where your website is live. Optionally add a one-sentence note about what you built.

steps.title: Write the brief
steps.body:
You are about to build a real website. The first move is not code. It is clarity. In one or two sentences, write what your site is about and who it is for.

Pick something you actually care about: a portfolio, a landing page for a side project, a simple blog, a resume site. The best project is one that would be useful to you even if no one graded it.

A strong brief answers four questions: what is the site, who is it for, what do visitors need to see, and what is the vibe. If you include all four you will save yourself hours of rework later.

**Example brief:** *I run a dog walking service in Austin. My clients are busy professionals. I need a home with testimonials, services with pricing, about with certifications, and a booking page. Vibe: friendly, trustworthy, outdoorsy. Primary goal: book a walk.*

**Why the brief matters before a single line of code.** Claude can build a website in minutes. That is the good news. The challenging news is that Claude will build whatever you describe — vague descriptions produce generic websites that look like every other template, while specific descriptions produce sites that feel intentional and personal. The brief is how you communicate specificity. Every word you invest in the brief saves five rounds of iteration later.

**How to write the what.** One sentence: *This is a [type of site] for [specific purpose].* Portfolio site for a freelance motion designer. Landing page for a SaaS product that helps restaurant managers track staff schedules. Blog covering ultramarathon training for people over forty. The more specific the purpose, the more targeted the design and copy Claude will produce.

**How to write the who.** Describe your visitors like you are briefing a designer: not demographics, behavior. *Busy professionals who make decisions quickly and will not read walls of text. They are already warm — they know me or were referred — so I do not need to convince them I am legitimate, I need to make booking easy.* That description tells Claude exactly what to optimize for: clarity over volume, booking flow over testimonials.

**How to describe the vibe.** Vibe is harder to describe but critical. Compare two vibe descriptions: *Professional* (generic, applies to everything) versus *Clean white space with one brand color, photography-forward, minimal text, zero stock photo energy* (specific, actionable). If you have reference sites you like, list their URLs. Claude can use them as design direction. Three reference URLs are worth a thousand adjectives.

**The brief prompt.** When you are ready to start, open Claude Code and paste: *I am building a website. Here is my brief: [paste your four-question answers]. Before you write any code, read this brief back to me in your own words to confirm you understood it. Then ask me any clarifying questions that would prevent rework later.*
steps.done_when: You have one or two sentences written down that answer what, who, pages, and vibe.

steps.title: Install Claude Code
steps.body:
Open your terminal. Install Claude Code with npm. Create a new folder for your project. Start Claude inside that folder.

First run will ask you to authenticate in the browser. That is normal. Sign in, come back to the terminal, and you are connected.

**What Claude Code actually is.** Claude Code is Claude running inside your terminal with direct access to your file system. It can read, write, and run files. It can install packages, run your local development server, open the browser, and read error messages. You describe what you want in plain English and Claude writes the code, runs it, reads the errors, and fixes them — without you needing to read a single line of code.

**Troubleshooting the install.** If `npm install -g @anthropic-ai/claude-code` fails with a permissions error on macOS, you need to fix npm's global install path. The cleanest fix: tell Claude Code the exact error message you see and it will give you the right command for your system. Do not search for fixes online — every system is slightly different and forum answers are often outdated.

**What to do during the first authentication.** Claude Code will print a URL in the terminal. Copy it and paste it into your browser. You will see an Anthropic sign-in page. Sign in with your Claude account. The browser will confirm authentication. Go back to your terminal — Claude is now connected. You will see a prompt waiting for your first message.

**Verifying the setup before moving on.** After Claude starts, type: *Tell me what folder I am in, what operating system you see, and whether you have access to create and modify files here.* Claude will respond with the folder path, OS, and confirm file access. If the folder path is wrong, type `exit`, navigate to the right folder, and run `claude` again. Always confirm you are in the right folder before building anything — Claude Code creates files wherever it is running.

**The first message to send.** Once you are confirmed in the right folder: *I am going to build a website here. This folder is empty right now. Before I paste my brief, confirm that you can create files in this location and tell me what tech stack you will use for a simple static website — just HTML, CSS, and JavaScript, no frameworks unless I ask for them.*
steps.done_when: Claude Code is running in your project folder and responding to prompts.

code.value:
npm install -g @anthropic-ai/claude-code
mkdir my-website
cd my-website
claude

steps.title: Get a plan before any code
steps.body:
Paste your brief into Claude Code. Then add one non-negotiable sentence: *Describe the structure and tech you recommend before writing any code so I can review the plan.*

Claude will come back with a plan: pages, tech stack, design direction. Compare every line to your brief. If you asked for minimal and white space, and the plan has particle backgrounds and dark gradients, catch it now. Name what to remove and what to replace it with. Vague feedback like *make it simpler* wastes rounds.

Fixing a plan takes 30 seconds. Fixing code takes 30 minutes.

**What a good plan looks like.** A plan should cover five things: (1) the pages you will have and what goes on each one, (2) the file structure (which files will be created and what they contain), (3) the tech stack and why (for a simple site: plain HTML/CSS/JS, no framework, one Google Font, no build step), (4) the color palette and typography — even one or two sentences, (5) any interactive elements and how they will work. If the plan is missing any of these, ask for it before approving.

**How to review the pages.** Open your brief and check each page Claude planned against your requirements. If your brief said you need a booking form and the plan does not include one, say: *Add a booking form to the Contact page with fields for: name, email, preferred date, and service type. The form should submit to a Formspree endpoint so it works without a back end.* Specific addition, specific implementation. If the plan includes a page you did not ask for, say: *Remove the Blog page — I did not ask for it and I want to keep this simple for now.*

**How to review the tech stack.** For a first website, you want the simplest possible stack: HTML, CSS, JavaScript. No React, no Next.js, no build steps. Every complexity added to the stack is a new failure mode to debug. If Claude proposes a framework, say: *Use plain HTML, CSS, and JavaScript only. No npm, no build steps. I need to be able to open the index.html file in a browser and have it work.* Keep it simple until you have shipped the first version.

**How to review the design direction.** The plan should describe the visual approach in at least one sentence. If Claude says *modern, clean design with blue accents* and your brief said *photography-forward with minimal text*, say: *The design should lead with large full-width images. The color palette should come from the photos, not override them. No blue. Primary background is white or off-white. One accent color, used sparingly.* Design direction is hard to express in code; catch it here.

**The approval message.** When the plan matches your brief: *This plan looks right. Proceed to build all the files now. Create the complete site — every page, all the CSS, any JavaScript needed for interactive elements. Do not stop after one file and ask for approval — build the whole thing and tell me when it is ready to preview.*
steps.done_when: Claude has shown you a plan and you have reviewed or corrected it until it matches your brief.

steps.title: Build and preview
steps.body:
Once the plan is right, tell Claude: *Let us build it. Create all the files.* Claude writes the HTML, CSS, and maybe JavaScript.

Then ask how to preview it locally. Claude will walk you through it. When your site opens in the browser for the first time, take the moment. You described something, and now it exists.

**What Claude will do during the build.** Claude will create files one by one and describe each one as it goes. You do not need to read the code. You need to know what was created. When Claude is done building, ask: *List every file you created, its location, and what it contains in one sentence each.* Read that list. Confirm it matches the plan — the right number of pages, the CSS file, any images or assets. If something is missing, say: *You did not create [file]. Create it now.*

**How to open the preview.** For a plain HTML site: ask Claude: *What is the exact command to open index.html in my default browser from the terminal?* Claude will give you the command for your operating system. On macOS it is typically `open index.html`. On Linux it is `xdg-open index.html`. On Windows it is `start index.html`. Run the command. Your browser opens the site.

**What to look at first in the preview.** Open the site and check three things before giving any feedback. First: does it open without errors? If the browser shows a blank page or an error, ask Claude: *The page opened blank / showed this error: [paste error]. Fix it.* Second: does the layout look roughly like what you described? Not perfectly — roughly. Is the structure there? Third: does the navigation work? Click every link. Every link should go somewhere, even if the destination page is a placeholder.

**What to do if the site will not open.** The most common reason a site will not open locally: a file path that works on one system but not another. If clicking a navigation link shows a 404, the links probably use forward slashes where they should not, or vice versa. Tell Claude: *Clicking the [page name] link shows a 404 / blank page. The link is [paste the href from the navigation]. Fix the path so it works when I open index.html directly from the file system without a server.*

**Taking the moment.** You described something in plain English. Claude wrote the code. You ran one command. Now you are looking at a website that did not exist thirty minutes ago. That is the capability you are building: the ability to turn descriptions into working software. This is the foundation of every project in this track.
steps.done_when: Your site renders in the browser on localhost.

steps.title: Iterate until you are proud
steps.body:
Look at the preview. Give targeted feedback. Name the element, describe the problem, suggest the direction.

Good: *The heading is lost against the dark background image. Make it white and add a subtle dark overlay so the text always pops.*

Bad: *The design needs work.*

Expect 5 to 15 rounds of feedback. The goal is not perfection. It is a site you would send to a friend.

**The anatomy of good feedback.** Every piece of feedback you give Claude should contain three parts: what element you are talking about, what the problem is, and what direction to take it. Element: *The hero section.* Problem: *The background image has text over it but the text is unreadable against the busy photo.* Direction: *Add a semi-transparent dark overlay (rgba 0,0,0,0.5) between the image and the text so the heading is always readable regardless of the photo.* That feedback produces a precise fix. Compare it to *the hero looks bad* — which produces a guess.

**How to give feedback on color.** Do not say *the color feels off.* Say: *The blue is too saturated — it feels corporate. Replace it with this hex value: #4A90A4. Also check that text on the blue background passes WCAG contrast — the white text should have at least a 4.5:1 ratio.* If you do not know the hex value you want, describe the feeling: *The blue should feel like a calm ocean, not a brand guideline. Desaturate it and lighten it slightly.* Give Claude a direction to move, not just a problem to solve.

**How to give feedback on typography.** Typography feedback is where beginners usually struggle — it is hard to name what feels wrong. Common issues and how to name them: *The body text feels too small and cramped* → *Increase body font size to 18px and set line-height to 1.7 for better readability.* *The headings feel heavy and overpowering* → *Reduce the H1 font-weight to 400 (regular) and the H2 to 500. The bold is too much for this light layout.* *The text feels like walls* → *Limit paragraph width to 65 characters (max-width: 65ch) and increase spacing between paragraphs to 1.5em.*

**What to check on every page.** After each round of changes, check: (1) navigation — do all links still work? Changes to file names sometimes break links, (2) mobile — resize your browser window to about 400px wide and check that the layout does not break, (3) images — are they loading? Do they look sharp or pixelated?, (4) forms — if you have a contact form, can you fill it out without anything looking broken? You do not need to submit it yet.

**Knowing when to stop iterating.** The goal is not perfection. It is a site you would confidently send to someone whose opinion matters to you. A useful test: imagine you just met someone relevant to your site's purpose (a potential client, a hiring manager, a collaborator) and you want to share your website. Would you hesitate because of something you see? If yes, fix that thing. If no, stop iterating and deploy.
steps.done_when: You have a site you are proud enough to share.

steps.title: Deploy to the internet
steps.body:
Ask Claude Code: *Help me deploy this website to the internet for free.* Claude walks you through the options. Vercel is the fastest path for a static site. Netlify and GitHub Pages also work.

When you see your site at a real URL, not localhost, take another moment. You built this.

**The three deployment options.** Vercel is the fastest for a first deployment: no account setup needed for the CLI, one command to deploy, you get a vercel.app URL immediately. Netlify is nearly as fast and gives you drag-and-drop as a fallback if the CLI gives you trouble. GitHub Pages requires a GitHub account and is slower to set up, but gives you a github.io URL which some people prefer. Tell Claude which you want, or ask Claude to recommend based on your situation.

**The Vercel deployment prompt.** Tell Claude: *Deploy this site to Vercel. Walk me through every step. I may not have Vercel CLI installed — start by checking and installing it if needed. Give me the exact commands to run and tell me what each one does before I run it.* Claude will check for the CLI, install it if needed, guide you through the login step (which opens a browser like Claude Code's first run), and then run the deploy command. The whole process takes about three minutes.

**What to check after deployment.** When Vercel gives you a URL, open it on your computer and on your phone. Check: does the site load? Does every navigation link work? Do images load? Does the layout look right on mobile? The mobile check is critical — a layout that looks perfect at desktop size can break at phone size. If the mobile layout is broken, tell Claude: *The mobile layout is broken — on a phone screen the [element] is [describe the problem]. Fix the CSS so it works on both desktop and mobile.* Then redeploy.

**How to redeploy after changes.** Once you have deployed to Vercel, redeploying is one command. Ask Claude: *What is the exact command to redeploy to the same Vercel URL after I make changes?* Claude will give you the command. For Vercel it is typically `vercel --prod`. Save that command in your README so you never have to ask again.

**Getting a custom domain later.** Your vercel.app URL is real and shareable. When you want a custom domain (yourname.com), Vercel makes it a one-screen setup in the dashboard. You buy the domain from any registrar (Namecheap, Google Domains, Porkbun), add it in the Vercel dashboard, and update the DNS settings at your registrar. The whole process takes about fifteen minutes and costs about twelve dollars a year. You do not need to do this now — the vercel.app URL is sufficient for this project.
steps.done_when: Your site is reachable at a public URL that you can paste into a browser on your phone.

steps.title: Ship it
steps.body:
Grab the live URL. Paste it into the submission form below.

You just built and shipped a real website with Claude Code. The workflow you used here — describe, plan, build, review, iterate, deploy — is the same workflow you will use for every project going forward.

**What you actually learned.** You did not just build a website. You learned a workflow for turning descriptions into working software. That workflow — brief, plan, build, iterate, deploy — scales to every complexity level. A landing page follows the same workflow as a web application. The conversations get longer, the files multiply, the debugging gets more specific, but the sequence is identical. What you learned today is not beginner knowledge you will leave behind. It is the foundational pattern.

**The skill you are building.** The most valuable thing that happened in this project was not the code Claude wrote. It was you learning to communicate precisely with Claude about what you want. The feedback you gave in the iterate step — naming the element, describing the problem, suggesting the direction — that is a skill. It gets faster and more precise with every project. Developers who learn this skill early ship dramatically faster than those who do not. You started building it today.

**What to build next.** Now that you know the workflow, the constraint is ideas, not ability. You can build: a landing page for your side project, a portfolio page, a simple web app that does one useful thing, a tool that solves a problem you have. The next project takes less time than this one because you already know how to set Claude up for success. The project after that takes less time than the second. The time-per-project curve drops fast.

**The prompt to start your next project.** When you are ready to build something new: *I am starting a new project in this folder. The site is: [brief]. Here is the plan I want you to follow before writing any code: [paste your brief format]. Describe the structure and tech before starting.* You have a reusable workflow now. The brief format, the plan-first requirement, the targeted iteration feedback, the deploy step — all of it transfers. You are a builder.
steps.done_when: You have the public URL ready to submit.
